speaker: interviewee
text: It came out of his depression, and so--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'll let you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: So our furniture was slowly sold out. And I can tell you from this moment on, I have no feeling for collecting as I was collecting in Vienna. What you see here, I feel like camping. Because whatever I brought to America of good things, I left my daughter. We bought the property together in San Rafael. And I left it to her. I have no feeling anymore for furniture, for antiques. Because I remember how I left them and how I sold them because I was forced to do. And what the feeling I got now is for the animals, for cats, for dogs, and for plants, for living things. Anyway, see, my husband had worked out to sell our furniture and things. In meantime, a lot of other immigrants came through the country. And they had to sell, too. So my husband took furniture and carpets from other people on consignment. And this worked very well. And we had a little bit of income, because it came handy. My income was very slowly going down. There was no wool more, no-- you see, the wool in Palestine came from France and Germany. And as the war broke out, France and Germany were occupied with other things, as in sen-- sending wool to Palestine. And there was no wool anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we thought, what are we doing now? Because even the things my husband got on consignment came to an end. There came no boats with furniture anymore. So everything was stopped. And we were standing again for a very serious decision.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had an idea. In one of the main streets of Tel Aviv that would be compared to market like it was 20 years ago, yeah, not the market of [INAUDIBLE] of today. And there was a store in the best neighborhood who sold typewriters. And he had a typewriter, one typewriter, in a big showcase.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I met this guy at the party. And I said to him, Klaus, you are really a fool. You have one typewriter in a big showcase. You pay a girl to take your orders. He lived mostly off repairs, yeah? So you have one girl there who take orders by telephone, yeah? That's a foolish idea. I give you an offer. I pay half your rent. You give me your showcase. And I take your orders. And you don't need the girl. He said, could you start tomorrow? [LAUGHS] I said, not tomorrow, but on, on Monday. So, I got a beautiful place. Now, he told-- he asked me, what would you sell? I said, I have an idea. In Israel-- that means it was still Palestine, there is the habit whenever you are invited, you never come with empty hands. And whatever you bring-- a piece of chocolate or a book or a flowers-- it's like a prize stamp on it, yeah? I would like to open a store for [NON-ENGLISH]. That means little gifts, yeah? And I will have different things. And I think I will make bookkeeping what sells best. And after two months, I will make this what only sells best. And so I started. And it was really hard to find merchandise, yes? But I had a few pounds from them from the sale of, of our furniture and our household goods. And I really started this business. And I decorated nicely. And I took his orders. And he was very glad and very content that it worked out so nice. And I found out after two months what sell best is bijoux, some necklace or a pin or pair of earrings-- much better the hostess was more-- I see-- glad to get it than piece, box of chocolate. So how do you get these things? And now, the war has started. There was nothing to buy. And as I had a hobby as a girl, I made always necklaces. So I went to Bethlehem. And I found an old Arab who was very nice. And I told him what I want to do. And he-- I said, I would like to buy rosary, rosaries, all kind of rosaries, yeah? And I will make necklaces out of it. Do you mind? He said, no. Business is business. [LAUGHS] He had no religious-- I mean, no idea that is a-- first of all, he was no Christian. Anyway, I bought a few rosaries, different things. And I bought some kind of grains, you see. I don't know. In German, it's [SPEAKING GERMAN] How would you say? This-- they are brown sticks. I don't know what you call it in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't know either. Anyway, I bought different grains. And I made necklaces of rosaries and of grains for sporty dresses. They looked very nice. And they sold perfect.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a few months, I decided only to have bijouterie. And I started to manufacture. And this was really the beginning of our wealth in Israel. I, I left this guy because I needed a bigger showcase. And I opened a store opposite, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I even made in the a newspaper a competition. We pay, I think, 200 pounds for someone for a good name. And the name come out Bijou. So then it's a store was Bijou. And we had-- it worked rather well. On the second-- and in the store, my husband worked. My husband was an excellent salesman. If there would be a story to sell me a beer in Munich, he would do it. So he sold these necklaces and earrings because I had no patience.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, when a woman came in and said, what earrings should I wear? What should I wear to pants, I would be angry and say, a moustache, you see? I really had no patience for this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in this time on another party, I met a guy of the Foreign Trade Institute in Israel, yeah, in Palestine. And he said, I know your stores and know what you are doing. Make a collection for export. That did it. And he introduced me. Well, see, the same trouble we had, all the neighboring Arab countries had. They got no merchandise. That was exactly the same trouble. There were no boats coming with anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he introduced me to buyers from Tehran, from Baghdad, from Cairo. And I got beautiful orders. And it worked perfectly well. Because for me, it was interesting. I found in the retail shop what is the taste of the people. I was still new in the country, yes? But I saw what sells.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this, I made in the collection for abroad. And the director of the Palestine Export Institute, he told me, do you know, in all my practice I see the first time that Arabs send blank checks like they're sending to you and leave it to you to choose merchandise. And we are very happy with you. In this sentence, I was you. It's likewise. So anyway, it worked nicely. And the retail shop worked. And my husband was a good sales, sales guy. And so we made a very nice living. Now comes a funny story, too. I always wanted to go to Egypt. And a Jewish woman from Palestine couldn't get an entry visa. And I was sorry for one thing. It was a very big department store in Cairo, Cicurel. It is like in Paris, in Paris Galeries Lafayettes or something. And they were my biggest clients. And they were even Jews. And they couldn't help me for a visa. And I had a-- we had a friend who came every Saturday to us for breakfast because his wife didn't want him to make crumbs on the carpet when there is no maid in. So he came to us for breakfast. And during breakfast he said, you know, in next months, I have a very interesting tip, a journey. The Palestine [INAUDIBLE] Corporation is invited for by King Farouk. And we are 14 men going. And I'm really enjoy-- looking forward. And I said, and you know, we will go 14 men and one woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, how come? You can't. You are not a member. I said, Walter, it's not your first lie and not your last. You will say I'm a member, like two years. And so I got my entry visa to Cai-- to Egypt and went with Sam to Egypt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was not only interesting concerning my profession, in any other way, too. I spent days in the Egyptian museum. And I found out that the necklaces Nefertiti were wearing were similar to these things that I did. And we never met. [LAUGHS] We have never met.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, I was very happy in Cairo. And one day, King Farouk invited us to a party. He went every night with his suite, with his followers, to a night, a night club, [INAUDIBLE] And he made a very smart decision for a king. He bought the place. So wesaw the money he is spending comes in back again. So anyways, there was a reception. And I was introduced. And he said, do you know? It's funny. He said, a young, attractive woman is collecting stamps. I think at your age, you have other hobbies. So I said, you know, one hobby doesn't exclude other hobbies. One can make different things. So anyway, he said, how come that you are interested in stamps? I said, I inherited from my father a very valuable collection. And he had special pieces. He said, tell me. What pieces did he have that's special?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had as much idea of stamps, I don't know. I had no idea at all. And I said, you know, it's very hard for me to speak about it. My father said when you speak about it, there comes the evil eye. Oh, and he said, oh, stop it. Stop it! Don't speak one word. I believe in evil eyes. And so I was saved. Because I had no idea from every valuable piece. Anyway, it was a lovely party. In the end, he kissed my hand. And I thought, I'm the only Jewish woman of Palestine whose hand was kissed by King Farouk. And this is the story of the Egyptian trip that was-- we went later to look so. And it was really very, very interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: So business went very well. I sold to all places. I had customers in Persia and in a-- all, all the Arabs, they were forced to buy. Because it's like nothing came in. And there were war and there were parties, yeah? And the women want to dress normally. And I was again on the bitter end. The rosaries were bought out. I got no material. I didn't know what to do. But what I do for necklaces? I made a lot of filigree jewelry and silver. And I made the designs. I was a very good designer. And it worked really well. Only I sold even to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the disadvantage is if I made on millimeter papier, paper, [INAUDIBLE] sketches it was hard for the Yemenites to do it. Because the Yemenites are like artists, you know. They have to make-- two add the personal note. And the American buyers didn't understand they are slight, slight differences. And I wasn't in America, anyway. But we-- I sent-- I explained by letter. They are artists. They can't do exactly the same, even if they have the sketches. There are slight differences. That was a one part of the what we exported. But what really-- the big business was Arab countries. And I had no material. So in Israel now comes-- and you will really laugh-- in Israel, is a big factory of noodles and macaroni. And they make different shapes-- little [NON-ENGLISH]. How do you, would you say-- round things and like shells and bows.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to this factory. And I said, what are you doing with all this material that got rotten when Israel-- a hot climate-- when you have worms in it and it's rotten? He said, I throw it away. I said, what would you give it to me? I say, I buy it. What will you do with it? I said, I make a try. I don't-- I can't you you how much I need. Give me a few, a few days and make a try. I took a, a big bag with different designs. And I went to Rehovot. Rehovot is a institute for research, yeah? Weizmann.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Weizmann Institute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Weizmann Institute, yes. And I said, could you help me? Could you impregnate these noodles, that they don't break and the no worms come in. What do you want to do with it? I said, jewelry. The guy thought I'm nuts. But he said, possibly. I've seen a lot of nutty women. But I shall oblige. And he found out what he could do. And I took these prepared noodles. And I found a very nice German [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That means she is not really an artist, you see-- half an artist. And I told her what I wanted. I wanted, for instance, the noodles that were paint outside were like shells, painted from ivory to a light pink in all shades. And we made beautiful necklaces. And I went with her to the Foreign Trade Institute with a collection of noodles. And they said, oh, it's an artisan collection. And we got orders on and on. Because the people in Egypt, in Baghdad, in wherever, there were parties, and there were balls. And the women had nothing to wear. And they bought this thing. There were not very expensive, you see. Because the noodles, I got for nothing. I had only to pay the, the work for rearranging it, yes, and make necklaces, and of course, what I want to gain. But I didn't do it for pleasure only.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it worked very well. And this worked really till the end of the war. And after the war, things came in. And we manufactured things there. Can I go back just a little bit? How did you perceive the end of the war? How did you know the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, there was the radio, yeah? We were hearing. That was no television yet, but we were hearing to radio every, every moment. And there was already the idea that there will be Palestine in the newspapers, yes-- Israel in the papers, that we'll be a Jewish state. And there's a very touching thing. My mother was what you would call Victorian. She was very unhappy in Israel. Because for after her opinions, there must be a special potato for a potato salad. And she couldn't get it. And she told me, imagine where I bought coffee, he didn't wash his hands before he served herring to a woman. And she was so disgusted. And she said, you know, the first boat that goes, I go back. And then came in the newspapers this story, there will be a Jewish state. And there were be Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], Jewish soldiers and a Jewish army and Jewish police.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was awfully excited. And she said, do you know, when these poor persecuted people get all this, I'll stay. And she kept her word. She passed away a few months later. The state of Israel was declared. And she passed away a few moments later. So she kept her word. What else would you like to know? How was it in Israel after the state in 1948? You're speaking of 1948, is that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was-- there was still-- we were, I was still in the same business, yeah? And I thought, well, I need things [INAUDIBLE] brought. So I went to Paris. And I went to Austria. And I bought merchandise, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I imported it. And it was very hard, because see, you couldn't import as much as you wanted. And there was heavy duty on it, yeah? But here again, God blessed me. I had an idea. I said to people, I had this business in Austria. And very honest women were hiding materials in Austria. So I have still the materials. They are my property. They are [INAUDIBLE]. May I bring it? Sure. And you wouldn't pay duties, wouldn't pay duty, because it's yours, yeah? So I-- but give me proof. Now, what proof could I give him? I went to a printing press and ordered old letters, Mrs. And Mr. Kimel, [INAUDIBLE] bijouterie export-import. That was very easy. That was my proof. I, I couldn't give him any other proof. But he was satisfied.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I brought a lot of merchandise. And I sold it with a very nice gain in the store and in the retail store. And it worked very well. So and on the other hand, in the meantime, Ben-Gurion had arranged with [INAUDIBLE] some arrangement for a refund of-- oh, not refund. Let's say, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Restitution.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- restitution. So my husband got money from Germany. And we had money saved. And I had, in the meantime, against the will of my husband-- he was in this time in the hospital-- bought a plot out of Tel Aviv. And I bought for it 50, 50 pounds. I didn't have the pounds. I had 10. And I bought it from friends and said I will pay it back. And what I did later.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband was very mad. And he said, only woman can buy a plot where you-- that you can't reach. There is no road goes there. And where have you bought it? I saw it on a map in the bank. [LAUGHS] Only a woman could do such a nonsense.

speaker: interviewee
text: The nonsense came out later that for the 50 English pounds, it was a value later of 100,000 [INAUDIBLE]-- 100,000 [INAUDIBLE] pounds. It was the best neighborhood. My neighbor was Dayan and Golda Meir. I told you. I always go for the best neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the neighborhood? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's called University Quarters. And we had this plot. But I always liked flowers and gardens. But-- and I wanted to live outside of Tel Aviv. But of course, we had no money to buy a build-- a house. And anyway, it was after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't buy anything. You couldn't get a refrigerator. You couldn't get a stove, nothing. And not for, not for love and not for money. It wasn't there, yeah? So I saw an advertising in the from the Argentine consul. He had brought for his sister-in-law a California custom-built trailer, yeah? And the police didn't allow him to put it on where he wanted it. And he has to sell. And I was looking at it. And he said, I saw my dream coming true-- a gas stove, a refrigerator, air conditioned, beautiful furniture. It was nine meter long, the longest thing I've ever seen. And I said to the consul, how much is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I gave a price. I knew I could afford it. And I said, all right. I come with my husband. And my husband saw it. And he said, over my dead body. I don't live here. I'm not a gypsy. I don't live-- I don't live in a sink like that. I said, I don't think you should live. I think at the weekend, we should use it. We have a nice apartment in San Francisco-- in Tel Aviv. But for the weekend, it would lovely. No. No. No. Man says no.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I spoke with the Argentine consul. And I said, would you give me one month to convince my husband? He said, sure. One month is not the end of the world. After one month, I said to my husband, I tell you one thing. I don't want to fight with you. But I paid in advance this consul a sum. If you don't want it, we forget it. We lost already money under Hitler. If we lose again, what could happen? I don't want that marriage suffers about. I don't know which-- how much I told him I had paid. He said, if you paid already, take it. That's nonsense. So we took it. He was the proudest owner lately-- later. Because we lived there. And it was very-- only weekends or vacation. It was a drive from, let's say, from our store, 20 minutes. It was later one of the best neighborhoods, what I told you. So we had this trailer. And after a couple of years again, when money came from Germany regularly, we had the idea to build there a house. And we sold the trailer. And the trailer had still a future. A archaeological company bought it and took it to the desert. And we bought-- we made on the same plot a house. This house-- as my father built plan-- build houses, I was used to the plans, yes? Before I could read, I understand the plan for a house, yes? So I made a plain what I really wanted. And we made a beautiful home there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as my husband and I suffered very much from the climate of the heat, we had sold already the store. And we were always six months in Europe in Bel-- in Germany or in Switzerland or in Austria and had rented the house in the meantime to consulates. And from the rent, we could live perfect in Europe, you see. The rent was very high, because it was a good neighborhood. And it's beautiful furnished. And everyone liked it. And so, so was no trouble. And we made a living in Europe, very comfortable. [DRINKS] And I think '65-- we were always from May till end of September in Europe. In May, I think '65, we were in Baden-Baden. And I met my second husband. Both my husbands were gamblers. But they played-- they gambled on different tables. And I was looking over--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still married to your first husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: To your first husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was looking at the, at the nicer guy. And I, I liked-- he wa-- I was some way attracted, yes? And he said, let's dinner together, my wife and you and your husband. And so we had dinner together and the next time, lunch together. And we spent a couple of days together. And after three or four days, he told me, do you know, I've thought things over. You have nothing in common with husband. I have nothing in common with my wife. Let's get married.
